The **bass clarinet** is a [musical instrument](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument") of the [clarinet](/wiki/Clarinet "Clarinet") family. Like the more common [soprano B♭ clarinet](/wiki/Soprano_clarinet "Soprano clarinet"), it is usually pitched in B♭ (meaning it is a [transposing instrument](/wiki/Transposing_instrument "Transposing instrument") on which a written C sounds as B♭), but it plays notes an [octave](/wiki/Octave "Octave") below the soprano B♭ clarinet.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Bass clarinets in other keys, notably C and A, also exist, but are very rare (in contrast to the regular A clarinet, which is quite common in classical music). Bass clarinets regularly perform in [orchestras](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra"), [wind ensembles and concert bands](/wiki/Concert_band "Concert band"), and occasionally in marching bands, and jazz bands. They also play an occasional solo role in contemporary music and jazz in particular.

Someone who plays a bass clarinet is called a bass clarinettist or a bass clarinetist.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bass_clarinet&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

Most modern bass clarinets are straight-bodied, with a small upturned silver-colored metal bell and curved metal neck. Early examples varied in shape, some having a doubled body making them look similar to [bassoons](/wiki/Bassoon "Bassoon"). The bass clarinet is fairly heavy and is supported either with a neck strap or an adjustable peg attached to its body. While [Adolphe Sax](/wiki/Adolphe_Sax "Adolphe Sax") imitated its upturned metal bell in his design of the larger [saxophones](/wiki/Saxophone "Saxophone"), the two instruments are fundamentally different. Bass clarinet bodies are most often made of [grenadilla](/wiki/Grenadilla "Grenadilla") (African Blackwood) or (more commonly for student-instruments) plastic resin, while saxophones are typically made of metal. (Metal bass clarinets exist,[[4]](#cite_note-4) but are rare.) More significantly, all clarinets have a [bore](/wiki/Bore_(wind_instruments) "Bore (wind instruments)") that is basically the same diameter along the body. This cylindrical bore differs from the saxophone's conical one and gives the clarinet its characteristic tone, causing it to [overblow](/wiki/Overblowing "Overblowing") at the twelfth (octave + fifth) compared with the saxophone's octave.

A majority of modern bass clarinets, like other clarinets in the family, have the [Boehm system](/wiki/Boehm_system_(clarinet) "Boehm system (clarinet)") of keys and fingering. However, bass clarinets are also manufactured in Germany with the [Oehler system](/wiki/Oehler_system "Oehler system") of keywork, which is most often known as the 'German" system in the US, because it is commonly used in Germany and Austria, as well as Eastern Europe and Turkey; bass clarinets produced with the Oehler system's predecessor, the [Albert system](/wiki/Albert_system "Albert system"), are still in use, particularly in these areas.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] German system bass clarinets frequently have a bore that is significantly narrower than most Boehm system instruments, resulting in a somewhat different sound and set of playing characteristics. Some modern clarinet makers have successfully produced Boehm system bass clarinets with a German bore, but these are not in widespread use.

Most modern Boehm system bass clarinets have an "extension" key allowing them to play to the (written) E♭. This key was originally added to allow easy transposition of parts for the relatively rare bass clarinet pitched in A, but it now finds significant use in concert band and other literature. A significant difference between soprano and bass clarinet key work is a key pad played by the left-hand index finger with a vent that may be uncovered for certain high notes. This allows a form of "half-hole" fingering that allows notes in higher registers to be played on the instrument. In addition, older bass clarinets have two register keys, one for middle D♯ and below, the other for middle E and higher. Newer models typically have an automatic register key mechanism, where a single left thumb key commands the two vent holes. Depending on whether the right hand ring finger (used in fingerings for middle D♯ and below) is down or up, the lower or upper vent hole will open.

Many professional or advanced bass clarinet models extend down to a low C, two octaves below written middle C. At concert pitch this note is the B♭ below the second ledger line below the bass staff or B♭1 in [scientific pitch notation](/wiki/Scientific_pitch_notation "Scientific pitch notation") (identical to the [bassoon](/wiki/Bassoon "Bassoon")'s lowest B♭). These three lowermost half-steps are played via additional keys operated by the right thumb, some of them often duplicated in the left- or right-hand little-finger key clusters. Overall, the instrument sounds an octave lower than the B♭ soprano clarinet.

As with all wind instruments, the upper limit of the range depends on the quality of the instrument and skill of the clarinetist. According to Aber and Lerstad, who give fingerings up to written C7 (sounding B♭5), the highest note commonly encountered in modern solo literature is the E below that (sounding D5, the D above treble C).[[5]](#cite_note-5) This gives the bass clarinet a usable range of up to four octaves, quite close to the range of the bassoon; indeed, many bass clarinetists perform works originally intended for bassoon or cello because of the plethora of literature for those two instruments and the scarcity of solo works for the bass clarinet.

## Uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bass_clarinet&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Uses")]

The bass clarinet has been regularly used in scoring for [orchestra](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra") and [concert band](/wiki/Concert_band "Concert band") since the mid-19th century, becoming more common during the middle and latter part of the 20th century.[[6]](#cite_note-6) A bass clarinet is not always called for in orchestra music, but is almost always called for in [concert band](/wiki/Concert_band "Concert band") music. In recent years, the bass clarinet has also seen a growing repertoire of solo literature including compositions for the instrument alone, or accompanied by [piano](/wiki/Piano "Piano"), orchestra, or other ensemble. It is also used in [clarinet choirs](/wiki/Clarinet_choir "Clarinet choir"), [marching bands](/wiki/Marching_band "Marching band"), and in [film scoring](/wiki/Film_score "Film score"), and has played a persistent role in [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz").

The bass clarinet has an appealing, rich, earthy and inky tone, quite distinct from other instruments in its range, drawing on and enhancing the qualities of the lower range of the soprano and alto instrument.

### Musical compositions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bass_clarinet&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Musical compositions")]

Perhaps the earliest solo passages for bass clarinet—indeed, among the earliest parts for the instrument—occur in [Mercadante's](/wiki/Saverio_Mercadante "Saverio Mercadante") 1834 opera *Emma d'Antiochia*, in which a lengthy solo introduces Emma's scene in Act 2. (Mercadante actually specified a glicibarifono for this part.) Two years later, [Giacomo Meyerbeer](/wiki/Giacomo_Meyerbeer "Giacomo Meyerbeer") wrote an important solo for bass clarinet in Act 4 of his opera *[Les Huguenots](/wiki/Les_Huguenots "Les Huguenots")*.

French composer [Hector Berlioz](/wiki/Hector_Berlioz "Hector Berlioz") was one of the first of the Romantics to use the bass clarinet in his large-scale works such as the *[Grande symphonie funèbre et triomphale](/wiki/Grande_symphonie_fun%C3%A8bre_et_triomphale "Grande symphonie funèbre et triomphale")*, Op. 15 (1840), the *[Te Deum](/wiki/Te_Deum_(Berlioz) "Te Deum (Berlioz)")*, Op. 22 (1849), and the opera *[Les Troyens](/wiki/Les_Troyens "Les Troyens")*, Op. 29 (1863). Later French composers to use the instrument included [Maurice Ravel](/wiki/Maurice_Ravel "Maurice Ravel"), who wrote virtuosic parts for the bass clarinet in his ballet *[Daphnis et Chloé](/wiki/Daphnis_et_Chlo%C3%A9 "Daphnis et Chloé")* (1912), *[La valse](/wiki/La_valse "La valse")* (1920), and his orchestration of [Modest Mussorgsky](/wiki/Modest_Mussorgsky "Modest Mussorgsky")'s *[Pictures at an Exhibition](/wiki/Pictures_at_an_Exhibition "Pictures at an Exhibition")* (1924).

The operas of [Richard Wagner](/wiki/Richard_Wagner "Richard Wagner") also make extensive use of the bass clarinet, beginning with *[Tannhäuser](/wiki/Tannh%C3%A4user_(opera) "Tannhäuser (opera)")* (1845). He incorporated the instrument fully into the wind section as both a solo and supporting instrument. Wagner pioneered in exploiting the instrument's dark, somber tone to represent sadness and melancholy. Wagner was almost completely responsible for making the instrument a permanent member of the opera orchestra. The instrument plays an extensive role in *[Tristan und Isolde](/wiki/Tristan_und_Isolde "Tristan und Isolde")* (1859), the operas of *[Der Ring des Nibelungen](/wiki/Der_Ring_des_Nibelungen "Der Ring des Nibelungen")* (1876), and *[Parsifal](/wiki/Parsifal "Parsifal")* (1882).

Also around this time, Hungarian pianist and composer [Franz Liszt](/wiki/Franz_Liszt "Franz Liszt") wrote important parts for the instrument in his symphonic poems *[Ce qu'on entend sur la montagne](/wiki/Ce_qu%27on_entend_sur_la_montagne "Ce qu'on entend sur la montagne")* (*What One Hears on the Mountain*), *Tasso*, and his *[Dante Symphony](/wiki/Dante_Symphony "Dante Symphony")*. [Giuseppe Verdi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Verdi "Giuseppe Verdi") followed suit, using it in Ernani, *[Aida](/wiki/Aida "Aida")* (1870), *[La forza del destino](/wiki/La_forza_del_destino "La forza del destino")*, *[Simon Boccanegra](/wiki/Simon_Boccanegra "Simon Boccanegra")*, *[Don Carlo](/wiki/Don_Carlo "Don Carlo")* and *[Falstaff](/wiki/Falstaff_(opera) "Falstaff (opera)")*. Following in Verdi's footsteps, [Giacomo Puccini](/wiki/Giacomo_Puccini "Giacomo Puccini"), composer of *[La Bohème](/wiki/La_Boh%C3%A8me "La Bohème")*, *[Tosca](/wiki/Tosca "Tosca")* and *[Madame Butterfly](/wiki/Madame_Butterfly "Madame Butterfly")*, used the bass clarinet in all of his operas, beginning with *[Edgar](/wiki/Edgar_(opera) "Edgar (opera)")* in 1889. The Russian composer [Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky](/wiki/Pyotr_Ilyich_Tchaikovsky "Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky") wrote some prominent solos for the instrument in his last ballet, *[The Nutcracker](/wiki/The_Nutcracker "The Nutcracker")*.

The later Romantics used the bass clarinet frequently in their works. All of [Gustav Mahler](/wiki/Gustav_Mahler "Gustav Mahler")'s symphonies include the instrument prominently, and often contain lengthy solos for the instrument, especially in his [Symphony No. 6 in A minor](/wiki/Symphony_No._6_(Mahler) "Symphony No. 6 (Mahler)"). [Richard Strauss](/wiki/Richard_Strauss "Richard Strauss") wrote for the instrument in all of his symphonic poems except for *[Don Juan](/wiki/Don_Juan_(Strauss) "Don Juan (Strauss)")*, and the instrument shared the spotlight with the tenor tuba in his 1898 tone poem, *[Don Quixote](/wiki/Don_Quixote_(Strauss) "Don Quixote (Strauss)")*, Op. 35. Strauss wrote for the instrument as he did for the smaller clarinets, and the parts often include playing in very high registers, such as in *Also Sprach Zarathustra*, Op. 30.

Composers of the [Second Viennese School](/wiki/Second_Viennese_School "Second Viennese School"), [Arnold Schoenberg](/wiki/Arnold_Schoenberg "Arnold Schoenberg"), [Anton Webern](/wiki/Anton_Webern "Anton Webern") and [Alban Berg](/wiki/Alban_Berg "Alban Berg"), often favored the instrument over the bassoon, the instrument's closest relative in terms of range. Russian composers [Dmitri Shostakovich](/wiki/Dmitri_Shostakovich "Dmitri Shostakovich") and [Sergei Prokofiev](/wiki/Sergei_Prokofiev "Sergei Prokofiev") used the low concert C and B♭ (equivalent to the bassoon's lowest two notes) in many of their compositions and an instrument with the extended range is necessary for works such as Shostakovich's Symphonies Nos. [4](/wiki/Symphony_No._4_(Shostakovich) "Symphony No. 4 (Shostakovich)"), 6, [7](/wiki/Symphony_No._7_(Shostakovich) "Symphony No. 7 (Shostakovich)"), [8](/wiki/Symphony_No._8_(Shostakovich) "Symphony No. 8 (Shostakovich)"), and [11](/wiki/Symphony_No._11_(Shostakovich) "Symphony No. 11 (Shostakovich)"), and [Leoš Janáček](/wiki/Leo%C5%A1_Jan%C3%A1%C4%8Dek "Leoš Janáček")'s [Sinfonietta](/wiki/Sinfonietta_(Jan%C3%A1%C4%8Dek) "Sinfonietta (Janáček)"). All of these works exploit the instrument's dark, powerful lower range.

Prokofiev wrote parts for the instrument in his Symphonies Nos. 2–7 and in his ballet *Romeo and Juliet*. [Sergei Rachmaninoff](/wiki/Sergei_Rachmaninoff "Sergei Rachmaninoff") used the instrument to great effect in his Symphonies Nos. [2](/wiki/Symphony_No._2_(Rachmaninoff) "Symphony No. 2 (Rachmaninoff)") and [3](/wiki/Symphony_No._3_(Rachmaninoff) "Symphony No. 3 (Rachmaninoff)") and in his symphonic poem, *[Isle of The Dead](/wiki/Isle_of_the_Dead_(Rachmaninoff) "Isle of the Dead (Rachmaninoff)")*. [Igor Stravinsky](/wiki/Igor_Stravinsky "Igor Stravinsky") also wrote complex parts for the instrument throughout his career, most prominently in his ballets *[The Firebird](/wiki/The_Firebird "The Firebird")* (1910), *[Petrushka](/wiki/Petrushka_(ballet) "Petrushka (ballet)")* (1911) and *[The Rite of Spring](/wiki/The_Rite_of_Spring "The Rite of Spring")* (1913).

The bass clarinet has a solo at the opening of the third movement of [Ferde Grofé](/wiki/Ferde_Grof%C3%A9 "Ferde Grofé")'s [Grand Canyon Suite](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_Suite "Grand Canyon Suite").

In the duet "[A Boy Like That](/wiki/A_Boy_Like_That "A Boy Like That")" from *[West Side Story](/wiki/West_Side_Story "West Side Story")* (1957), [Leonard Bernstein](/wiki/Leonard_Bernstein "Leonard Bernstein") scored for "the inky sounds of three bass clarinets".[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Early minimalist [Steve Reich](/wiki/Steve_Reich "Steve Reich")'s *[Music for 18 Musicians](/wiki/Music_for_18_Musicians "Music for 18 Musicians")* (1976) calls for two bass clarinets, featured prominently in the lower register. Used almost percussively, the effect of deep, staccato repetitions, played beneath a static rhythmic drone, is to create a feeling of slowly fluctuating cycles.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

One of the most performed works for Bass Clarinet is [Marc Mellits](/wiki/Marc_Mellits "Marc Mellits")'s Black, with over 3,000 performances as of 2024.

Many modern composers employ the bass along with the contra-alto and contrabass clarinets, such as [Esa-Pekka Salonen](/wiki/Esa-Pekka_Salonen "Esa-Pekka Salonen") in his [Piano Concerto](/wiki/Piano_Concerto_(Salonen) "Piano Concerto (Salonen)"). A great amount of literature can be found in the wind ensemble, in which there is always a part for the instrument.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

There are many important solo pieces, duos, sonatas and concertos for bass clarinet, including:

### Soloists and ensembles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bass_clarinet&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Soloists and ensembles")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Tara_Bouman.jpg/250px-Tara_Bouman.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tara_Bouman.jpg)

[Tara Bouman](/wiki/Tara_Bouman "Tara Bouman") playing bass clarinet

It was not until the 1950s that classical performers began to adopt the bass clarinet as their primary instrument. The pioneer was the [Czech](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic") performer [Josef Horák](/wiki/Josef_Hor%C3%A1k "Josef Horák") (1931–2005), who is credited as having performed the first ever solo bass clarinet recital on March 23, 1955.[[17]](#cite_note-17) This marked a turning point when the instrument first became thought of as a soloist's instrument.

Because the repertoire of solo music for the bass clarinet was quite small, most bass clarinet soloists specialize in new music, while also arranging works composed for other instruments from earlier eras (such as the [Bach](/wiki/Johann_Sebastian_Bach "Johann Sebastian Bach") [Cello Suites](/wiki/Cello_Suites_(Bach) "Cello Suites (Bach)")). Beginning with Horák, many players have commissioned works for the instrument, and consequently there now exists a repertoire of hundreds of solo works, many by prominent international composers such as [Brian Ferneyhough](/wiki/Brian_Ferneyhough "Brian Ferneyhough") and [David Lang](/wiki/David_Lang_(composer) "David Lang (composer)"). In addition to Horák, other specialist performers include [Henri Bok](/wiki/Henri_Bok "Henri Bok") (Netherlands), his student [Luís Afonso](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Afonso "Luís Afonso") (Brazil), [Dennis Smylie](/wiki/Dennis_Smylie "Dennis Smylie") (United States), Tommie Lundberg (Sweden), [Harry Sparnaay](/wiki/Harry_Sparnaay "Harry Sparnaay") (Netherlands, who has worked with important composers such as [Luciano Berio](/wiki/Luciano_Berio "Luciano Berio"), [Iannis Xenakis](/wiki/Iannis_Xenakis "Iannis Xenakis"), and [Morton Feldman](/wiki/Morton_Feldman "Morton Feldman")), Fie Schouten (artistic director Basklarinet Festijn; worked with o.a. [Georges Aperghis](/wiki/Georges_Aperghis "Georges Aperghis"), [Michael Finnissy](/wiki/Michael_Finnissy "Michael Finnissy"), [Theo Loevendie](/wiki/Theo_Loevendie "Theo Loevendie"), [Louis Sclavis](/wiki/Louis_Sclavis "Louis Sclavis"), [Rozalie Hirs](/wiki/Rozalie_Hirs "Rozalie Hirs")) [Jason Alder](/wiki/Jason_Alder "Jason Alder"), [Evan Ziporyn](/wiki/Evan_Ziporyn "Evan Ziporyn") (United States), and [Michael Lowenstern](/wiki/Michael_Lowenstern "Michael Lowenstern") (United States); the latter two are also composers.

In October 2005, the First World Bass Clarinet Convention was held in [Rotterdam](/wiki/Rotterdam "Rotterdam"), Netherlands, at which Horák was the guest of honor and played in one of the many concerts given by the leading bass clarinetists from around the world (including all the aforementioned performers, as well as many others).[[18]](#cite_note-18)

In April 2014, 70th birthday of [Harry Sparnaay](/wiki/Harry_Sparnaay "Harry Sparnaay"), the first Basklarinet Festijn was held in the Netherlands.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Initiators and artistic leaders are former students of Sparnaay and active in contemporary composed and improvised music: Fie Schouten and Tobias Klein.

At least two professional bass-clarinet quartets exist. Rocco Parisi's Bass Clarinet Quartet is an Italian group whose repertoire includes transcriptions of music by [Gioacchino Rossini](/wiki/Gioacchino_Rossini "Gioacchino Rossini"), [Niccolò Paganini](/wiki/Niccol%C3%B2_Paganini "Niccolò Paganini"), and [Ástor Piazzolla](/wiki/%C3%81stor_Piazzolla "Ástor Piazzolla"). [Edmund Welles](/wiki/Edmund_Welles "Edmund Welles") is the name of a bass clarinet quartet based in San Francisco. Their repertoire includes original "heavy chamber music" and transcriptions of [madrigals](/wiki/Madrigal_(music) "Madrigal (music)"), [boogie-woogie](/wiki/Boogie-woogie "Boogie-woogie") tunes, and [heavy metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music") songs. Two of the members of Edmund Welles also perform as a bass clarinet duo, Sqwonk.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### In jazz

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bass_clarinet&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: In jazz")]

While the bass clarinet was seldom heard in early [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") compositions, a bass clarinet solo by [Wilbur Sweatman](/wiki/Wilbur_Sweatman "Wilbur Sweatman") can be heard on his 1924 recording "Battleship Kate" and a bass clarinet solo by [Omer Simeon](/wiki/Omer_Simeon "Omer Simeon") can be heard in the 1926 recording "[Someday Sweetheart](/wiki/Someday_Sweetheart "Someday Sweetheart")" by [Jelly Roll Morton](/wiki/Jelly_Roll_Morton "Jelly Roll Morton") and His Red Hot Peppers. Additionally, [Benny Goodman](/wiki/Benny_Goodman "Benny Goodman") recorded with the instrument a few times early in his career.

[Harry Carney](/wiki/Harry_Carney "Harry Carney"), [Duke Ellington](/wiki/Duke_Ellington "Duke Ellington")'s [baritone saxophonist](/wiki/Baritone_saxophone "Baritone saxophone") for 47 years, played bass clarinet in some of Ellington's arrangements, first recording with it on the song, "Saddest Tale" in 1934. He was featured soloist on many Ellington recordings, including 27 titles on bass clarinet.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The first jazz album on which the leader solely played bass clarinet was *[Great Ideas of Western Mann](/wiki/Great_Ideas_of_Western_Mann "Great Ideas of Western Mann")* (1957) by [Herbie Mann](/wiki/Herbie_Mann "Herbie Mann"), better known as a flautist. However, avant-garde musician [Eric Dolphy](/wiki/Eric_Dolphy "Eric Dolphy") (1928–1964) was the first major jazz soloist on the instrument, and established much of the vocabulary and technique used by later performers. He used the entire range of the instrument in his solos. [Bennie Maupin](/wiki/Bennie_Maupin "Bennie Maupin") emerged in the late 1960s as a primary player of the instrument, playing on [Miles Davis](/wiki/Miles_Davis "Miles Davis")'s seminal record *[Bitches Brew](/wiki/Bitches_Brew "Bitches Brew")* as well as several records with [Herbie Hancock](/wiki/Herbie_Hancock "Herbie Hancock")'s [Mwandishi](/wiki/Mwandishi "Mwandishi") group. His style resembles Dolphy's in its use of advanced harmonies.

While the bass clarinet has been used often since Dolphy, it is typically used by a [saxophonist](/wiki/Saxophonist "Saxophonist") or [clarinetist](/wiki/Clarinetist "Clarinetist") as a second or third instrument; such musicians include [David Murray](/wiki/David_Murray_(saxophonist) "David Murray (saxophonist)"), [Marcus Miller](/wiki/Marcus_Miller "Marcus Miller"), [John Surman](/wiki/John_Surman "John Surman"), [John Gilmore](/wiki/John_Gilmore_(musician) "John Gilmore (musician)"), [Bob Mintzer](/wiki/Bob_Mintzer "Bob Mintzer"), [John Coltrane](/wiki/John_Coltrane "John Coltrane") (to whom Dolphy's mother left some of Dolphy's instruments including his bass clarinet.[[22]](#cite_note-22)), [Brian Landrus](/wiki/Brian_Landrus "Brian Landrus"), [James Carter](/wiki/James_Carter_(musician) "James Carter (musician)"), [Steve Buckley](/wiki/Steve_Buckley_(musician) "Steve Buckley (musician)"), [Andy Biskin](/wiki/Andy_Biskin "Andy Biskin"), [Don Byron](/wiki/Don_Byron "Don Byron"), [Julian Siegel](/wiki/Julian_Siegel "Julian Siegel"), [Gunter Hampel](/wiki/Gunter_Hampel "Gunter Hampel"), [Michel Portal](/wiki/Michel_Portal "Michel Portal"), [Myron Walden](/wiki/Myron_Walden "Myron Walden"), [Yusef Lateef](/wiki/Yusef_Lateef "Yusef Lateef"), [Paul McCandless](/wiki/Paul_McCandless "Paul McCandless"), [Gianluigi Trovesi](/wiki/Gianluigi_Trovesi "Gianluigi Trovesi"), and [Chris Potter](/wiki/Chris_Potter_(jazz_saxophonist) "Chris Potter (jazz saxophonist)"). Very few performers have used the instrument exclusively, but such performers include American [Michael Pinz](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Pinz&action=edit&redlink=1 "Michael Pinz (page does not exist)"), Berlin-based bass clarinetist [Rudi Mahall](/wiki/Rudi_Mahall "Rudi Mahall"), and French bass clarinetists [Louis Sclavis](/wiki/Louis_Sclavis "Louis Sclavis") and [Denis Colin](/wiki/Denis_Colin "Denis Colin"). Klezmer clarinetist [Giora Feidman](/wiki/Giora_Feidman "Giora Feidman") is known for idiosyncratic use of the bass clarinet on some klezmer and jazz tunes.

### In rock

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bass_clarinet&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: In rock")]

On [The Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles") 1967 recording of [George Harrison](/wiki/George_Harrison "George Harrison")'s "[It's All Too Much](/wiki/It%27s_All_Too_Much "It's All Too Much")", a prominent bass clarinet [ostinato](/wiki/Ostinato "Ostinato"), scored and conducted by producer [George Martin](/wiki/George_Martin "George Martin"), is played by session musician Paul Harvey.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Victor Hayden, AKA [The Mascara Snake](/wiki/The_Mascara_Snake "The Mascara Snake"), was a member of [Captain Beefheart](/wiki/Captain_Beefheart "Captain Beefheart")'s band in the late 1960s, playing bass clarinet on the 1969 album *[Trout Mask Replica](/wiki/Trout_Mask_Replica "Trout Mask Replica")*.

On [Steely Dan](/wiki/Steely_Dan "Steely Dan")'s 1980 song "[Babylon Sisters](/wiki/Babylon_Sisters "Babylon Sisters")", bass clarinets are played by George Marge and Walter Kane.

On [Flight of the Conchords](/wiki/Flight_of_the_Conchords "Flight of the Conchords")' 2009 song "[Rambling Through the Avenues of Time](/wiki/I_Told_You_I_Was_Freaky "I Told You I Was Freaky")", the instrument is used as a form of [word painting](/wiki/Word_painting "Word painting") when one is heard after [Bret McKenzie](/wiki/Bret_McKenzie "Bret McKenzie") sings the line "my heart played a bass clarinet". The part is played by David Ralicke.

On [Paramore](/wiki/Paramore "Paramore")'s 2023 album *[This Is Why](/wiki/This_Is_Why "This Is Why")*, the song "Figure 8" prominently features a bass clarinet. The part is played by Henry Solomon.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bass_clarinet&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Bass_clarinet_glicibarifono_Catterini_Bate.jpg/250px-Bass_clarinet_glicibarifono_Catterini_Bate.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bass_clarinet_glicibarifono_Catterini_Bate.jpg)

Glicibarifono by Catterini, 1838

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Museum_of_Fine_Arts%2C_Boston_-_Nicola_Papalini%2C_bass_clarinet_in_C.jpg/250px-Museum_of_Fine_Arts%2C_Boston_-_Nicola_Papalini%2C_bass_clarinet_in_C.jpg)](/wiki/File:Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston_-_Nicola_Papalini,_bass_clarinet_in_C.jpg)

The serpentine bass clarinet by Papalini, 1820

There are several instruments that can arguably be considered the first bass clarinet. Probably the earliest is a [dulcian](/wiki/Dulcian "Dulcian")-shaped instrument in the [Museum Carolino Augusteum](/wiki/Salzburg_Museum "Salzburg Museum") in [Salzburg](/wiki/Salzburg "Salzburg"). It is incomplete, lacking a [crook](/wiki/Bocal "Bocal") or mouthpiece, and appears to date from the first half of the eighteenth century. Its wide cylindrical bore and its fingering suggest it was a [chalumeau](/wiki/Chalumeau "Chalumeau") or clarinet in the bass range.[[25]](#cite_note-vdm1987-25) Four anonymous bass chalumeaux or clarinets apparently dating from the eighteenth century and having from one to six keys also appear to be among the earliest examples, and one in particular has been suggested to date from before 1750.[[26]](#cite_note-rendall-26) However, the authenticity of at least one of these instruments has been questioned.[[27]](#cite_note-young1981-27)

In the [Munich Stadtmuseum](/wiki/Munich_Stadtmuseum "Munich Stadtmuseum") there is an instrument made c. 1770 by the Mayrhofers of [Passau](/wiki/Passau "Passau"),[[27]](#cite_note-young1981-27) who are often credited with the invention of the [basset horn](/wiki/Basset-horn "Basset-horn"). It resembles early sickle-shaped basset horns, but has a larger bore and is longer, playing in low B♭. Whether this should be considered a low basset horn or a bass clarinet is a matter of opinion. In any case, no further work along this line is known to have been done.

A 1772 newspaper article describes an instrument called the *"basse-tube"*, invented by G. Lott in Paris in 1772.[[28]](#cite_note-28) This instrument has not survived and very little is known of it. The article has frequently been cited as the earliest record of a bass clarinet, but it has more recently been suggested that the basse-tube was in fact a basset horn.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The *Klarinetten-Bass* by [Heinrich Grenser](/wiki/Heinrich_Grenser "Heinrich Grenser"), c. 1793, had a folded, bassoon-like shape and an extended range, and was presumably intended to serve as a bassoon replacement in military bands. Desfontenelles of Lisieux built a bass clarinet in 1807 whose shape was similar to that of the later [saxophone](/wiki/Saxophone "Saxophone"). It had thirteen keys, at a time when most soprano clarinets had fewer.

Additional designs were developed by many other makers, including Dumas of Sommières (who called his instrument a *"Basse guerrière"*) in 1807; Nicola Papalini, c. 1810 (an odd design, in the form of a serpentine series of curves, carved out of wood); George Catlin of [Hartford, Connecticut](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut") (*"clarion"*) c. 1810; Sautermeister of Lyons (*"Basse-orgue"*) in 1812; Gottlieb Streitwolf in 1828; and Catterino Catterini (*"glicibarifono"*) in the 1830s.[[25]](#cite_note-vdm1987-25)[[26]](#cite_note-rendall-26)[[30]](#cite_note-30) These last four, and several others of the same period, had bassoon-like folded shapes, and most had extended ranges. A straight-bodied instrument without extended range was produced in 1832 by Isaac Dacosta and Auguste Buffet.[[25]](#cite_note-vdm1987-25)[[26]](#cite_note-rendall-26)

Finally, [Adolphe Sax](/wiki/Adolphe_Sax "Adolphe Sax"), a [Belgian](/wiki/Belgian_people "Belgian people") manufacturer of musical instruments, designed a straight-bodied form of the bass clarinet in 1838. Sax's expertise in acoustics led him to include such features as accurately-placed, large tone holes and a second register hole. His instrument achieved great success and became the basis for all bass clarinet designs since.

The instrument on which [Anton Stadler](/wiki/Anton_Stadler "Anton Stadler") first played [Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart](/wiki/Wolfgang_Amadeus_Mozart "Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart")'s [clarinet concerto](/wiki/Clarinet_Concerto_(Mozart) "Clarinet Concerto (Mozart)") was originally called a *Bass-Klarinette*, but was not a bass clarinet in the modern sense; since the late eighteenth century this instrument has been called a [basset clarinet](/wiki/Basset_clarinet "Basset clarinet").

## Notation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bass_clarinet&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Notation")]

Orchestral music for bass clarinet is written using one of four systems:

1. Conventional treble clef in B♭ (French notation). This sounds an octave and a major second lower than written and therefore uses the same fingerings as the soprano clarinet, and is the most common.
2. Bass clef in B♭ (German notation). This sounds a major second (tone, or whole step) lower than written.[[a]](#cite_note-31) For music written in bass clef, higher passages may be written in treble clef to avoid the use of excessive ledger lines, but this should not be confused with system (a), in which notes sound an octave lower than in system (b). It is therefore necessary to play the treble clef one octave higher than it would be played in French notation, so that it continues to sound a major second lower. Unlike music for the [bassoon](/wiki/Bassoon "Bassoon"), the [tenor clef](/wiki/Tenor_clef "Tenor clef") is not used for higher passages.
3. Bass clef in B♭ (Russian notation). This notation mixes the German and French systems. Music written in bass clef is played a major second lower than written (German), however when written in treble clef, it sounds a major ninth lower, so the player uses the fingerings they normally would when playing soprano clarinet (French).[[b]](#cite_note-32)
4. Bass clef in B♭ (Italian notation). This notation is written a major ninth higher than sounding pitch, as the French notation, however also uses bass clef. This means that the bass clef part would be read an octave lower than if reading it in German notation. The treble clef remains the same as French notation.[[c]](#cite_note-33)

Music is occasionally encountered written for the bass clarinet in A, e.g., in Wagner operas, and [Mahler](/wiki/Gustav_Mahler "Gustav Mahler") or [Rachmaninov](/wiki/Rachmaninov "Rachmaninov") symphonies. This music tends to be written in bass clef, although not invariably (e.g. [Ravel](/wiki/Maurice_Ravel "Maurice Ravel")'s *[La Valse](/wiki/La_Valse "La Valse")*). Probably the first bass clarinet in A was made by Johann Adam Henkel, around 1850, and a number of greater and lesser known makers continued to produce examples over the next 70 years: e.g., Stengel, Moritz, Kruspe, *et al*. Buffet made some instruments in the 1920s, but the bass in A was never produced in any great numbers, and after the 1920s very few instruments in A were made (although Fritz Wurlitzer experimented with one in the 1940s).[[31]](#cite_note-34)

Despite its relative rarity, important works by some prominent composers featured the bass clarinet in A, and in the early 20th century makers began regularly offering B♭ bass clarinets equipped with an E♭ extension key, so that bass parts in A could be transposed onto the B♭ instrument.

In the 1970s there was a mild revival of interest in the instrument, and Selmer of Paris produced a few instruments pitched in A with Boehm system key work and keyed to low E♭ (even though the original parts seldom descend below written low E). However, these instruments were expensive and many players were reluctant to haul around two heavy bass clarinets to rehearsals and performances, so few of the modern bass clarinets in A were sold. Selmer ceased production of the bass clarinet in A in the late 1980s. Today, very few players own a bass clarinet in A and these parts are generally played on the B♭ instrument, transposing them down a semitone.
